text: The surgeon divides the lesser omentum below the hiatus and to the right using a ligature, revealing the right corner of the diaphragm.
The surgeon divides the peritoneum anterior to the esophagus, extending the incision laterally to the left to expose the superior aspect of the left crura.
The surgeon further exposes the right crura and identifies the posterior vagus nerve, which is then transected using a ligature.
The surgeon sends a segment of the posterior vagus nerve, located above the right branch of the criminal nerve of Grassy, for frozen section analysis, confirming the presence of nerve fibers.

The surgeon identifies the anterior left vagus nerve and excises a segment using ligature. The excised segment is sent to pathology for frozen section analysis, confirming the presence of anterior vagus nerve fibers.
The surgeon visually inspects the surgical field for any additional branches of the vagus nerve, confirming that no further branches are present.
The esophagus, with an NG tube in place, is retracted to the left, allowing for posterior freeing of the esophagus and exposure of the left crus of the diaphragm.
The surgeon mobilizes the esophagus and moves it intra-abdominally. The hiatus is tightened using a single suture of 2-0 silk, visibly narrowing the opening to prevent further herniation.
The surgeon begins the subtotal gastrectomy, focusing on the stomach area for resection.

The surgeon divides the gastrocolic omentum near the greater curvature of the stomach, carefully preserving the gastroepiploic arcade. The dissection progresses from the lower antrum upwards to the hemigastrectomy site, located above the middle aspect of the greater curvature.
The surgeon performs dissection along the greater curvature of the stomach, extending towards the first portion of the duodenum, stopping just before the gastroduodenal artery (GTA).

The surgeon divides the lesser omentum using a precise technique, staying close to the gastric wall. The division extends to a point just above the first branch of the crow's foot and continues beyond the pylorus into the first portion of the duodenum.
The surgeon transects the first portion of the duodenum beyond the pylorus using an Endo GIA Echelon 60 stapler with blue staples. The transected duodenum and pylorus are then retracted superiorly.
The surgeon clears and divides adhesions attached to the posterior aspect of the stomach, ensuring a clear surgical field for further procedures.

The surgeon uses an endo-GIA echelon stapling device with green staples and peristrips to transect the stomach between two cleared areas from the omentum. Three applications of the stapler are made to completely divide the stomach.
The surgeon retracts the nasogastric tube to the gastroesophageal junction before proceeding with the division of the stomach.

The surgeon displaces the omentum and identifies the transverse colon, lifting it to reveal the ligament of Treitz. The proximal jejunum is then brought anterior to the colon and positioned near the remaining gastric pouch.

Two stay sutures are placed in a serosal muscular fashion between the jejunum and the posterior wall of the remaining gastric pouch, approximately two centimeters above the transected pouch, ensuring there is no tension on the sutures.
The proximal jejunum is positioned towards the greater curvature of the stomach, while the distal part is oriented towards the lesser curvature.

The surgeon creates an opening with electrocautery in the posterior gastric wall and the anterior jejunal antimesenteric wall. The endo-GIA echelon-sixty stapler with a blue cartridge is introduced into the jejunum and stomach, advanced, closed, and fired for fifteen seconds. The staple line is inspected, showing no signs of bleeding.

The surgeon closes the opening in the stomach and jejunum using continuous threo-PDS sutures, ensuring all layers are included. A second serosomuscular interrupted layer with threoethabond is applied to reinforce the closure of the defect.

The surgeon fixes the afferent loop of the gastrojejunostomy to the greater curvature of the stomach using an additional suture to prevent kinking. A similar suture is applied to the descending limb, ensuring stability and proper alignment.
The surgeon performs an air leak test on the anastomosis to ensure its airtight integrity, with no visible air leaks observed.
